channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Democrat Wins Mayoral Race in Republican-Leaning Waukesha, Wis.
item.description: Alicia Halvensleben, the president of the city’s Common Council, defeated a Republican legislator, continuing a string of Trump-era mayoral wins for Democrats.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Mustafa Hussain/Getty Images
item.media:description: Waukesha, Wis., is a city of about 70,000 people about 20 miles west of Milwaukee.

item.title: Lawmakers Greet Iran Cease-fire With Relief and More Questions
item.description: Democrats continued to raise serious questions about a path forward while Republican leaders were mostly mum on President Trump’s decision to de-escalate tensions.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Lawmakers of both parties were relieved at the announcement of a cease-fire though Democrats had grave questions about the path forward.

item.title: In Georgia’s 14th District Special Runoff, Democrats Pulled Off Their Biggest Shift Yet
item.description: Shawn Harris lost by about 12 percentage points in the 14th Congressional District, but he shifted the district 25 points to the left.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Soler and Christine Zhang

item.title: Chris Taylor, a Liberal Judge, Wins Race for Wisconsin Supreme Court
item.description: With Judge Chris Taylor’s win, liberals increased their hold on the court. Races for the Wisconsin Supreme Court often draw national attention, but not this year.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Jovanny Hernandez
item.media:description: Chris Taylor, a state appeals judge, was elected to the Wisconsin Supreme Court on Tuesday.

item.title: Inside the Race to Find a Downed U.S. Airman in Iran
item.description: Our reporter Helene Cooper walks us through the high-stakes operation to rescue a downed U.S. airman in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Gilad Thaler, Thomas Vollkommer, Laura Salaberry, Nikolay Nikolov, Nour Idriss, Ray Whitehouse and Paul Abowd

item.title: $35 Million or Your Money Back: One Candidate’s Silicon Valley Lifeline
item.description: Tech leaders see Matt Mahan, a moderate Democrat, as their best option in the California governor’s race, but tensions have risen as he has struggled to gain traction in polls.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Jeff Chiu/Associated Press
item.media:description: Matt Mahan has entered the California governor’s race as a Silicon Valley ally opposed to a billionaire’s tax.

item.title: Clay Fuller, Trump Ally, Will Take Marjorie Taylor Greene’s Seat in Georgia
item.description: Democrats had hoped for a strong showing, but the conservative district in Northwest Georgia elected Clay Fuller after President Trump endorsed him.
item.dc:creator: Richard Fausset and Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Alyssa Pointer/Reuters
item.media:description: Clay Fuller’s victory is a win for President Trump, who visited the district in February and appeared onstage with him.

item.title: Polls in Wisconsin Reflect a More Muted Supreme Court Race
item.description: This year’s election has featured more undecided voters and lower overall awareness compared with recent cycles.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Soler
item.media:credit: Joe Timmerman/Wisconsin Watch, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Ballot booths at Warner Park Community Recreation Center on in Madison on Tuesday.

item.title: In Phoenix, Turning Point and Jane Fonda fight for control of a utility.
item.description: The Salt River Project is little known beyond Arizona, but Tuesday’s elections at the giant public utility have brought in heavy hitters and tested Turning Point after Charlie Kirk.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Jonathan J. Cooper/Associated Press
item.media:description: A sign directing voters outside the headquarters of Salt River Project in Tempe, Ariz., last month.

item.title: Trump Backs Down, but Questions Remain Over Iran and the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump’s short-term intimidation may have worked, but the fundamental divides with Iran are as sharp as they were in February.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump during a White House news conference on Monday.

item.title: Leader of University of Wisconsin System Is Fired by the Board
item.description: Jay O. Rothman’s departure brought an end to a four-year stint as leader of the university system following a public struggle for power.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder and Stephanie Saul
item.media:credit: John Hart/Wisconsin State Journal, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Jay O. Rothman, as president of the Wisconsin university system, at a University of Wisconsin Board of Regents meeting in Madison last fall.

item.title: Michigan Woman Missing in the Bahamas After Falling Off a Boat
item.description: Lynette Hooker was traveling with her husband from Hope Town to Elbow Cay on Saturday when she went overboard, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor

item.title: Democrats Hope to Show Strength in Marjorie Taylor Greene’s Former District
item.description: The contest for Marjorie Taylor Greene’s former seat could continue a streak of special election results that have shown a significant shift to the left.
item.dc:creator: Christine Zhang and Alex Lemonides
item.media:credit: Mike Stewart/Associated Press
item.media:description: Shawn Harris, a Democrat, advanced to Tuesday’s runoff with the most votes in a crowded field that included 12 Republicans.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Julie Bosman

item.title: Trump’s Iran Threats Look Like Self-Incrimination for Potential War Crimes
item.description: President Trump, in vowing to systematically destroy civilian infrastructure and annihilate Iran’s entire civilization, appears to be creating evidence about his intentions.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump addressing reporters in a briefing on Monday at the White House, before his latest threat. Asked then whether he was concerned that the attacks he has threatened could amount to war crimes, Mr. Trump said: “No, I’m not.”

item.title: With Threat to Wipe Out Iran’s Civilization, Trump’s Rhetoric Goes Beyond Bluster
item.description: The president’s violent rhetoric risks damaging his credibility as a negotiator and the country’s standing in the world.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Norman Bussel, Who Chronicled Veterans’ Unseen Traumas, Dies at 102
item.description: After a grueling year in a German prison camp during World War II, he endured crushing nightmares and survivor guilt back home, leading him to spread the word about veterans’ suffering.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Iranians in L.A. Watch Fearfully as Trump Threatens a Civilization
item.description: Over half a million U.S. residents are at least partly of Iranian descent. They may have left Iran, but many treasure it still.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler and Orlando Mayorquín
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
item.media:description: West Los Angeles is the largest community of Iranians outside of their home country.

item.title: To Boost Military Budget, Trump Targets Popular Programs at Home
item.description: Amid the war with Iran, the president has proposed to scale back some of the very programs meant to ease families’ financial burdens.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Annie Karni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: For President Trump, the renewed debate over federal spending arrives at a perilous political and economic moment.

item.title: As Deadline Nears, Confusion Over U.S.-Iran Talks Swirls Worldwide
item.description: U.S., Iranian, Israeli and other officials offered varying accounts about the state of negotiations between Washington and Tehran.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump set an 8 p.m. Tuesday deadline for Iran to open the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Video Captures Enormous Warehouse Fire Raging Near Los Angeles
item.description: No one was killed or injured in the fire in Ontario, Calif., but a gigantic building was destroyed in an act of arson, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Emma Schartz

item.title: Venezuelan Doctor Detained in Texas After Losing Job to Visa Freeze
item.description: The Venezuelan doctor, who was forced out of his hospital job by a federal visa pause, was detained by Border Patrol on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I am a physician in a federally recognized medically underserved area. In an area like this, missing even a resident physician like me, is a lot,” Ezequiel Veliz said. “That means that hundreds of patients are not going to be seen.”

item.title: Chairman of the Joint Chiefs Struggles to Justify Trump’s Threats
item.description: The president’s apocalyptic rhetoric clashes with the responsibility of Gen. Dan Caine to protect the military’s honor.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: General Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, during a briefing on the war with Iran. He vowed that the force he leads would “always strike lawful targets.”

item.title: DOJ’s Civil Rights Division Investigates Cassidy Hutchinson, Who Testified Against Trump
item.description: It was a highly unusual move by Justice Department leadership to direct a case that appears to involve accusations of lying to Congress to a division that normally focuses on civil rights abuses.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Michael S. Schmidt
item.category: House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Cassidy Hutchinson during a hearing on Capitol Hill in 2022. The investigation into Ms. Hutchinson began weeks ago after the Justice Department received a referral from a Trump ally in Congress who accused Ms. Hutchinson of lying to the House committee that investigated Jan. 6.

item.title: Only Trump Knows Why Bondi Was Fired as Attorney General, Blanche Says
item.description: In his first news conference since being elevated to acting attorney general, Todd Blanche said that “nobody has any idea” what led to Pam Bondi’s dismissal other than President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, now the acting attorney general, and Pam Bondi, who was dismissed from the role by President Trump, at the White House in January.

item.title: Newlywed Wife of U.S. Soldier Released From Immigration Detention
item.description: The couple had gone to the husband’s Army base to complete paperwork so they could move in together. But within hours that plan derailed, and New York Times reporting about the case quickly spread.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Lily Brooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Staff Sgt. Matthew Blank and his wife, Annie Ramos, after she was released from immigration detention in Basile, La., on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Threatens Broad Attacks in Iran. How Is Illegal Conduct in War Defined?
item.description: It is illegal for any military to target civilians, as President Trump has suggested he would in threats against Iran. But the U.S. has sought significant leeway in defining a civilian target.
item.dc:creator: Thomas Gibbons-Neff
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Airstrikes demolished Shahid Beheshti University in Tehran. The U.S. military’s law of war manual states that “the protection of civilians against the harmful effects of hostilities is one of the main purposes of the law of war.”

item.title: ICE Agents Shoot Into a Car, Injuring a Suspect in Northern California
item.description: A federal official said the agents were pursuing a wanted gang member who tried to run one of them over. Dash cam video complicates the account.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla and Hamed Aleaziz

item.title: Child Finds a Human Skull During Easter Egg Hunt in a California Park
item.description: The authorities said they were examining the skull, which a child found in a park in Long Beach on Sunday, to identify the remains.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser

item.title: U.S. Strikes Kharg Island as Trump Raises Iran Threats to Apocalyptic Levels
item.description: U.S. forces launched more than 90 “restrikes” on Kharg Island, Iran’s oil export hub, early on Tuesday but have avoided hitting oil infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Julian E. Barnes and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Planet Labs Pbc/AFP, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Kharg Island in February before the U.S. and Israeli strikes. The United States launched a series of more than 90 strikes on the island, an Iranian oil hub, early Tuesday.

item.title: ‘Definitely a Sham’: As Tariffs Climb, Trade Fraud and Accounting Tricks Proliferate
item.description: U.S. imports from China have shrunk drastically. But billions of dollars of the change appear to be the result of accounting gimmicks and outright fraud.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times

item.title: 6 Takeaways From the Story of Trump’s Decision to Go to War With Iran
item.description: New details from the weeks leading up to the campaign show how President Trump’s alignment with Benjamin Netanyahu and a lack of sustained opposition from his inner circle put the United States on a course to war.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel and President Trump at Mar-a-Lago, his club and residence in Florida, in December.

item.title: How Trump Took the U.S. to War With Iran
item.description: In a series of Situation Room meetings, President Trump weighed his instincts against the deep concerns of his vice president and a pessimistic intelligence assessment. Here’s the inside story of how he made the fateful decision.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan and Maggie Haberman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: The decision by President Trump to give the go-ahead to join Israel in attacking Iran was influenced by a presentation by Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in February that led to a series of discussions inside the White House over the following days and weeks.

item.title: What to Watch in the Georgia Special Election to Replace Marjorie Taylor Greene
item.description: Clay Fuller, a Republican allied with President Trump, will face Shawn Harris, a Democrat, in the election to fill the remainder of Ms. Greene’s term after her resignation from Congress.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Photographs by Megan Varner/Getty Images
item.media:description: There is little question that Clay Fuller, left, a local district attorney, is likely to win, but Democrats are hoping Shawn Harris, right, can trim the Republican margin of victory again.

item.title: Fact-Checking Republicans’ Misleading Claims About Mail-In Ballots and Fraudulent Voting
item.description: President Trump, his administration and G.O.P. lawmakers have claimed widespread issues with mailed ballots and fraudulent voting, but the evidence doesn’t support them.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.media:credit: Bridget Bennett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Documented cases of fraud in mailed ballots are extremely rare, according to election officials.

item.title: ‘No Labels’ Arizona Wants to Rebrand as the Independent Party
item.description: Both the Democratic and Republican parties in Arizona have been locked in a legal battle with a chapter of the group “No Labels” as it tries to rechristen itself.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Johnson is chairman of a new Arizona political group that sought to name itself the Independent Party.

item.title: Without Elon Musk, Wisconsin’s Supreme Court Justice Election Goes Quiet
item.description: Elections for the Wisconsin Supreme Court have previously brought record-breaking spending and national attention. Tuesday’s race has been a more muted affair.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Jovanny Hernandez
item.media:description: Candidates for the Wisconsin Supreme Court, Maria Lazar, left, and Chris Taylor, during a debate this month.

item.title: Texas Considers Required Reading List for Schools, Which Includes the Bible
item.description: Education officials are planning an overhaul to English and social studies in the nation’s largest Republican led state.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Mervosh
item.media:credit: Jay Janner/The Austin American-Statesman, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A proposed book list in Texas includes readings from the Bible, such as the definition of love from First Corinthians and the story of David and Goliath.
